content.problemStatement:
Chef is setting up a perfect bath for himself. He has XXX litres of hot water and YYY litres of cold water.

The initial temperature of water in his bathtub is AAA degrees. On mixing water, the temperature of the bathtub changes as following:


The temperature rises by 111 degree on mixing 111 litre of hot water.
The temperature drops by 111 degree on mixing 111 litre of cold water.
Determine whether he can set the temperature to BBB degrees for a perfect bath.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of four space-separated integers A,B,X,A, B, X,A,B,X, and YYY — the initial temperature of bathtub, the desired temperature of bathtub, the amount of hot water in litres, and the amount of cold water in litres respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, `YES` if Chef can get the desired temperature for his bath, and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated integers A B X Y — the initial temperature, the desired temperature, the amount of hot water (in litres) and the amount of cold water (in litres).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Chef can obtain the desired temperature, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Need +1 degree, X=2 hot litres → possible.
Test case 2: Already at desired temperature.
Test case 3: Need -10 degrees, but only 9 cold litres → impossible.
Test case 4: Need +1 degree, but X=0 hot litres → impossible.
1) Increase by 20, X=20 → YES. 2) Decrease by 5, Y=5 → YES. 3) Increase by 2, but only 1 hot litre → NO.
First case already matches. Second case needs -3 degrees, Y=3 → YES.

solution.approach: For each test case compute D = B - A. If D >= 0 we need to increase the temperature by D degrees, which requires at least D litres of hot water. Hence the condition is D <= X. If D < 0 we need to decrease by -D degrees, requiring at least -D litres of cold water, i.e. -D <= Y. When D == 0 no water is needed, answer is always YES. The check is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
